The surgeon begins creating a surgical flap from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the medial umbilical ligaments.
The surgeon proceeds with medial dissection in the surgical field, focusing on separating tissue layers in the area of the inguinal hernia.
The surgeon exposes the bone during the hernia procedure, revealing its surface within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection, moving away from the previously dissected medial area, focusing on separating tissue layers in the lateral region.
The surgeon sweeps cremasteric tissue laterally, moving it away from the surgical field.
The surgeon anticipates the appearance of the cord structures, which become visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, which is visible in the surgical field during the hernia procedure.
I had some students in the room for this case, so I'm just pointing out anatomical structures to them and explaining the process of the dissection.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, which visibly includes some preperitoneal fat being drawn along with it.
The surgeon begins parietalizing the cord structures, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visually exposes the femoral defect, highlighting its location and appearance in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection down to the outer hyperspace, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures, ensuring the mesh can sit flat against the sidewall without clamshelling, and further parietalizes down to the psoas muscle.
The surgeon displays the anatomy of the myopectineal orifice and proceeds to search for a cord lipoma within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies fatty tissue extending into the inguinal canal and visualizes the iliopubic tract adjacent to it.
The surgeon observes herniated fat protruding into a separate defect, located lateral to the internal ring.
The surgeon removes the tissue from the surgical field, completing the dissection in the outer hyperspace area.
The surgeon continues to sweep back tissue, revealing that it is part of the nodal tissue overlying the iliac vessels.
The surgeon encounters increased bleeding during the dissection, with visible blood pooling in the surgical field.
The surgeon decides to leave the nodal tissue in place after reducing the herniated fat that was protruding into the inguinal canal, visible near the iliopubic tract.
The surgeon places a 16x12 mesh over the myopectineal orifice to cover the hernia defect.
The surgeon adjusts the mesh over the myopectineal area and proceeds to close the flap, ensuring the mesh is securely positioned.
